note: Taniguchi, Takagi, Otsuka, Hayashi, Hamada, 'Collective Predictive Coding as Model of Science: Formalizing Scientific Activities Towards Generative Science', arXiv:2409.00102v1 [physics.soc-ph], 27 Aug 2024 (23p). Purely CONCEPTUAL/THEORETICAL framework paper (CPC-MS). Central thesis exactly matches C063: CPC-MS 'models science as a decentralized Bayesian inference process carried out by a community of agents' (Abstract), extending the individual predictive-coding / free-energy principle to a social/collective process via a probabilistic graphical model (shared external representation w = published papers/theories/consensus; internal z^k; observations o^k; per-agent bias theta^k). Peer review is explicitly mapped onto the accept/reject step of the Metropolis-Hastings Naming Game (MHNG): Fig 2 caption says scientific activity involving peer review is 'structurally analogous to the MHNG'; the worked drug-X/disease-Y example (Sec 2.4 step 4) says the reviewer's decision 'corresponds to the Listener's acceptance judgment in MHNG'; Sec 4.1 gives an explicit MH acceptance ratio min(1, P(z^k|theta^k,w^l)/P(z^k|theta^k,w^k)) for paper acceptance. So C064 (peer-review-as-Metropolis-Hastings) is ACCURATE in substance but is stated in the manuscript WITHOUT a \citep -> IMPRECISE-OK, add \citep{taniguchi2024cpc}. C065/C066 are background pointers: shared memory w (= global scientific representation) and collective/decentralized validation via peer review are genuinely the paper's content, and the paper is squarely about the social layer of science (social objectivity, community-as-holder-of-knowledge) -> both ACCURATE. Reverse caveats: (a) MH is presented as a STRUCTURAL ANALOGY/MODEL, not a literal claim that reviewers compute an MH ratio; (b) NO simulation is run (listed as future work), so CPC-MS is a proposed framework, not a demonstrated/implemented mechanism; (c) there is no named single 'collective validator' module -- validation is distributed across the reviewer population.

C063.evidence: Manuscript L96 (CHAR): '\citet{taniguchi2024cpc} propose Collective Predictive Coding (CPC-MS) as a social model of science, recasting scientific activity as decentralized Bayesian inference across a community.' Fully supported and is the paper's central thesis. Title: 'Collective Predictive Coding as Model of Science'. Abstract: 'This paper proposes a new conceptual framework called Collective Predictive Coding as a Model of Science (CPC-MS) ... CPC-MS models science as a decentralized Bayesian inference process carried out by a community of agents. The framework describes how individual scientists' partial observations and internal representations are integrated through communication and peer review to produce shared external scientific knowledge.' Sec 1: 'Crucially, CPC-MS posits that shared external representations -- scientific knowledge in this context -- are updated through a form of peer-review process that functions as a decentralized Bayesian inference [Tan24].' The SOCIAL framing is explicit (Sec 3.1 'Social Objectivity': 'The holders of scientific knowledge ... are the scientific community as a whole rather than individual scientists', aligning with the social approach to scientific knowledge [Lon90, Kit93]). Formalized as a PGM (Fig 3, Table 1) with shared external representation w, internal z^k, observations o^k. Every term in the clause (CPC-MS; social model of science; decentralized Bayesian inference; community of agents) maps verbatim to the paper.

C064.evidence: Manuscript L96 (UNCITED, adjacent clause traced to CPC): 'peer review is a Metropolis-Hastings consensus process.' The substance is ACCURATE -- the paper explicitly models peer review as the accept/reject step of the Metropolis-Hastings Naming Game (MHNG). (1) Sec 2.1: CPC rests on 'the mathematical fact that a certain type of language game ... i.e., Metropolis-Hastings naming game (Figure 2), can be acted as a decentralized Bayesian inference of a shared latent variable' [TYM+23]. (2) Fig 2 caption: 'scientific activities updating shared explicit knowledge through experiments and communications involving peer-review process. The total systemic dynamics is structurally analogous to the MHNG.' (3) Sec 2.2/2.4 step 4 'Judgment of Scientific Representations': a submitted paper w is accepted/rejected by reviewers, and (Sec 2.4) 'This corresponds to the Listener's acceptance judgment in MHNG, and when done properly, the distribution of accepted papers can be viewed as samples from q(w | {o^k_d}_k).' (4) Sec 4.1 gives an explicit MH acceptance ratio for paper acceptance between agents: 'in MHNG, w^k_d is accepted with probability min(1, P(z^k_d|theta^k, w^l_d) / P(z^k_d|theta^k, w^k_d))'. (5) Sec 3.1 invokes MCMC convergence conditions on the peer-review chain (ergodicity, i.i.d. sampling, reducibility/'blind spot' theories, periodicity). The ONLY defect is a MISSING citation: the manuscript states this without \citep, though it clearly traces to CPC. Minor precision nuance: the paper frames it as a structural analogy/model, so 'is a Metropolis-Hastings consensus process' is marginally strong vs. 'is modeled as'.
C064.fix: Add \citep{taniguchi2024cpc} to the clause. Optionally soften the copula to reflect the paper's analogy framing, e.g. 'peer review is modeled as a Metropolis-Hastings acceptance (consensus) process~\citep{taniguchi2024cpc}' -- since the paper presents peer review as structurally analogous to the MHNG accept/reject step, not as reviewers literally computing an MH ratio.

C065.evidence: Manuscript L249 (Discussion, BG): 'a collective validator (extension to multi-agent collective with shared memory w)~\citep{taniguchi2024cpc}.' As a background pointer this is accurate. 'Shared memory w' maps exactly to CPC-MS's shared external / global scientific representation w: Sec 2.2 'we call w global scientific representations ... w represents a paper or a theoretical argument'; Table 2 (Nomenclature) 'w_d = External representation for the d-th target'; Fig 3 shows w as the shared parent node inferred across agents. 'Multi-agent collective' = the community of K agents performing decentralized Bayesian inference (Fig 3, Sec 2.2). 'Collective validator' = the collective peer-review/replication process by which the community accepts/rejects w: Sec 3.1 'individual biases ... get corrected in critical dialogues including peer reviews and replications', and 'such mutual criticisms can be seen as an essential part of the decentralized Bayesian inference ... which realizes the inference to the posterior distribution at the scientific community as a whole.' Caveat (does not undermine the cite): the paper has no single named 'collective validator' module -- validation is DISTRIBUTED across reviewers via the MHNG accept/reject step -- so 'a collective validator' is the manuscript's own reification/label for that distributed process.
C065.fix: None required. (Optional, LOW: note that CPC-MS realizes collective validation as distributed peer review over the shared representation w rather than a single validator module.)

C066.evidence: Manuscript L251 (Discussion, BG): 'the single-agent framing's social layer is abstracted away by SERVO~\citep{taniguchi2024cpc}.' Accurate as a pointer to the source of the 'social layer' idea. taniguchi2024cpc is precisely about the social/collective layer of science that a single-agent framing omits: it extends individual predictive coding / FEP ('basically, the idea of PC and FEP are about individual cognitive processes', Sec 1) to a COLLECTIVE/social process ('the CPC hypothesis was proposed for symbol emergence ... a generative model of symbol emergence in human society'), and Sec 3 is devoted to social dynamics (Sec 3.1 Social Objectivity: knowledge held by 'the scientific community as a whole rather than individual scientists'; consensus formation through communication/peer review among many agents). Citing it as the reference for the social layer that SERVO (single-agent) abstracts away is a faithful background attribution.

reverse_findings.fact: The Metropolis-Hastings mapping is presented as a STRUCTURAL ANALOGY / model, not as a literal empirical claim that reviewers compute an MH ratio. Fig 2 caption: peer-review dynamics are 'structurally analogous to the MHNG'; Sec 2.4 step 4: the review decision 'corresponds to the Listener's acceptance judgment in MHNG'; the explicit acceptance ratio min(1, P(z^k_d|theta^k,w^l_d)/P(z^k_d|theta^k,w^k_d)) in Sec 4.1 is used illustratively (to argue AI-human representational divergence yields low acceptance / poor convergence), not as a description of how human reviewers actually decide.
reverse_findings.loc: Abstract; Sec 2.1 (MHNG); Fig 2 caption; Sec 2.4 step 4; Sec 4.1 (MH acceptance ratio)
reverse_findings.mismatch: Manuscript C064 clause 'peer review is a Metropolis-Hastings consensus process' uses an equivalence copula ('is'); the paper frames it as an analogy/model ('structurally analogous to', 'corresponds to'). Recommend 'is modeled as / structurally analogous to'. Direction is correct; refinement only.

reverse_findings.fact: The paper is entirely CONCEPTUAL/THEORETICAL: no experiments, no simulation, and no empirical validation are performed. 'Simulation Study of CPC Science Model' is explicitly listed as FUTURE work (Sec 5): 'by executing this model on a computer, it is possible to simulate how the scientific community produces knowledge' -- i.e., not yet done. Sec 6 lists 'conducting empirical studies to test its validity' as future work. Sec 2.2 states CPC-MS 'does not necessarily provide a method enabling the agents to reach the objective truth, but rather provides a view of scientific communication.'
reverse_findings.loc: Sec 5 (Future Work: Simulation Study); Sec 6 (Conclusion); Sec 2.2 (l. 'does not necessarily provide a method')
reverse_findings.mismatch: Any manuscript usage implying CPC-MS supplies an implemented/validated mechanism (e.g., reading 'a collective validator~\citep{taniguchi2024cpc}' as a demonstrated component) would overstate; CPC-MS is a proposed conceptual framework, not a demonstrated system. As used (background/conceptual pointer for C065/C066) this is fine; flagged so the SERVO framing does not lean on it as an empirical result.

reverse_findings.fact: There is no single named 'collective validator' entity in the paper. Validation is DISTRIBUTED across the reviewer population: acceptance/rejection is the MHNG accept/reject step performed by individual reviewers, and 'the distribution of accepted papers can be regarded as samples of q(w | {z^k}_k)' (Sec 2.2 step 4). The shared knowledge store is the global scientific representation w (published papers/established theories/consensus models; Table 1, Table 2), held by the community as a whole (Sec 3.1).
reverse_findings.mismatch: Manuscript C065 'a collective validator' reifies a single validator; CPC-MS distributes validation across reviewers (decentralized Bayesian inference). The shared-memory-w half of the claim is exact; the 'validator' half is a reasonable but slightly reifying gloss. No correction required for a BG cite.
